topic: Error Handling - Custom Error Enums with Display

turns.content: I'm trying to build a low-level networking engine in Rust, and I keep thinking of error handling like how exceptions work in C++ or try-catch blocks in Java. But I've heard about these custom enums instead of just using standard errors, so what's actually better?

That's a great starting point. In Rust we don't use exceptions at all; instead we use the Result type or our own multi-variant error enums with Display and Debug traits implemented. These custom enums let you define specific variants like NetworkTimeout, InvalidConfig, or ConnectionRefused without needing to allocate heap memory every time.
Okay so it's basically a switch statement but with types? I'm worried about the verbosity though. Isn't there some boilerplate everywhere when you define these enums compared to just using Result?

You're right that there is some setup, but once you have the enum defined with Display and Debug traits implemented, it becomes much more flexible than plain Result. For example if your network layer can return multiple different failure reasons, a single enum covers them all instead of wrapping many separate Result types.
Exactly! And what about converting errors from other sources? Like when I call an external crate function that returns std::io::Error, how do I map those into my custom enum?

You use the From trait to implement automatic conversions. By deriving or manually implementing From<ExternalError> for your custom Enum, you can pass through errors using the ? operator without needing explicit match statements in every function call.

So if I have a function that returns a custom error type and another one that takes std::io::Error as input, I just implement From for the second case? That sounds convenient but is it really worth the effort?

It's absolutely worth it when you're building reusable libraries. The ? operator combined with From impls eliminates boilerplate and keeps error propagation clean throughout your codebase. It also avoids the pitfalls of manual error unwrapping which can lead to runtime panics if not handled properly.

I see. But I'm still concerned about memory allocation. If every error variant is a struct, won't that cause allocations when errors are created? I want zero-cost abstractions like in C where we don't allocate for control flow.

Actually, Rust custom error enums designed this way are typically no-alloc by default. The variants themselves are just data structures without extra heap allocation unless you specifically choose to store large payloads in them. This is a key advantage over some other languages where every exception might carry stack frames or large objects.
Wait, so if I don't add any fields beyond the basic error codes, it's truly zero-cost? Can you give me an example of a minimal enum with Display and Debug implemented that doesn't allocate?

Sure. You can define something like pub enum NetworkError { Timeout, ConnectionRefused, InvalidAddress } and derive both Display and Debug automatically. The compiler generates the implementations for you without needing to manually write out any code, so there's no runtime overhead or extra memory usage.
That's really neat. But what if I need something more complex? Like when an error contains a string message or a nested structure? Does that break the no-alloc idea?

If you include strings or other heap allocations in your enum variants, then yes, those specific instances will allocate. But for most performance-critical paths, you can avoid storing large data directly and instead store references to error codes or indices into a global registry of messages.
Oh I see! So the enum itself stays small but external strings are stored elsewhere? And how do I handle that in my code when I want to display the full message?

You'd implement Display for your custom error type to iterate over the variants and fetch messages from a lookup table. This way you avoid duplicating string data everywhere while still getting human-readable output when errors are displayed.
This is starting to make sense. But I'm still confused about the difference between using Result<T, E> vs a custom enum with From impls. When exactly should I choose one over the other?

Use plain Result when you only have two outcomes and don't need to distinguish many specific error conditions. Choose custom enums when you need fine-grained control over how different failure modes are reported, especially if you're building a library where users will query detailed error information.

Got it. So for my networking engine, I should probably go with a custom enum since there are many distinct network errors like DNS failures or socket timeouts. And I can use From to integrate with the standard library io errors.

Exactly right. That combination gives you both flexibility and safety. You get detailed error reporting without paying the price of manual error unwrapping or exception handling overhead, which is perfect for performance-sensitive code.
